U2L5 - Feature Selection and Regulariation

Type date here

Front Matter

#Delete all objects from Environment - Use with caution
remove(list = ls()) 

#Load Libraries
library(tidyverse)

library(gridExtra)

## Attaching package: 'gridExtra'

library(ISLR2)
library(leaps) #Not used before (may need to install)
library(glmnet) #Not used before (may need to install); used for LASSO/Ridge
## Loading required package: Matrix

##     expand, pack, unpack

## Loaded glmnet 4.1-10

Example - Illustrating the Bias-Variance Tradeoff

User defined functions for this problem

f = function(x) {
  #This is the true regression function
  x ^ 2
}

gen_sim_data = function(f, sample_size = 100) {
  #This function randomly generates a dataset of sample_size observations using y = f(x) + epsilon
  x = runif(n = sample_size, min = 0, max = 1) #randomly generate x's
  eps = rnorm(n = sample_size, mean = 0, sd = sqrt(0.5625)) #randomly generate noise
  y = f(x) + eps #generate y's using the regression form
  tibble(x, y)
}

get_mse <- function(truth, estimate) {
  #This function calculates the MSE 
  mean((estimate - truth) ^ 2)
}

get_bias <- function(estimate, truth) {
  #This function calculates the bias
  mean(estimate) - truth
}

get_var <- function(estimate) {
  #This function calculates the variance 
  mean((estimate - mean(estimate)) ^ 2)
}

Randomly generate a dataset; plot dataset and the true regression form

#Randomly Generate a Dataset
set.seed(1)
sim_data <- gen_sim_data(f)

#Plot the dataset and the true regression function
SimulatedData <- 
  ggplot(data = sim_data, mapping = aes(x = x, y = y)) +
  geom_point(alpha = 0.35, size = 2) +
  geom_line(data = data.frame(x = seq(0, 1, 0.001), 
                              y = f(seq(0, 1, 0.001))),
            mapping = aes(x = x, y = y),
            linewidth = 1)

SimulatedData

Plot Each estimated line

#Fit the models
fit_0 = lm(y ~ 1,                   data = sim_data)
fit_1 = lm(y ~ poly(x, degree = 1), data = sim_data)
fit_2 = lm(y ~ poly(x, degree = 2), data = sim_data)
fit_9 = lm(y ~ poly(x, degree = 9), data = sim_data)

#Create plots of the estimated models
#Plot of Degree 0 model
degree0 <-
  SimulatedData + 
  geom_line(data = data.frame(x = seq(0,1,0.001), y = predict(fit_0, data.frame(x=seq(0,1,0.001)))), color = "orange", linetype = "dashed",
            linewidth = 1) +
  labs(title = "Degree 0")

degree0

degree1 <-
  SimulatedData + 
  geom_line(data = data.frame(x = seq(0,1,0.001), y = predict(fit_1, data.frame(x=seq(0,1,0.001)))), color = "red", linetype = "dashed",
            linewidth = 1) +
  labs(title = "Degree 1")

degree1

degree2 <-
  SimulatedData + 
  geom_line(data = data.frame(x = seq(0,1,0.001), y = predict(fit_2, data.frame(x=seq(0,1,0.001)))), color = "blue", linetype = "dashed",
            linewidth = 1) +
  labs(title = "Degree 2")

degree2

degree9 <-
  SimulatedData + 
  geom_line(data = data.frame(x = seq(0,1,0.001), y = predict(fit_9, data.frame(x=seq(0,1,0.001)))), color = "purple", linetype = "dashed",
            linewidth = 1) +
  labs(title = "Degree 9")

degree9

Create one plot showing all four models

gridExtra::grid.arrange(degree0, degree1, degree2, degree9, ncol = 2)

Illustrating Variance

#Illustrate Variance for Highly Flexible (degree = 9) Model
set.seed(123)
plot_list <- list()
for(i in 1:4){
  sim_data <- gen_sim_data(f)
  
  fit_temp = lm(y ~ poly(x, degree = 9), data = sim_data)
  
  plot_list[[i]] <-
  ggplot(sim_data, mapping = aes(x=x, y = y)) +
    geom_point(alpha = 0.35) + 
    geom_line(data = data.frame(x = seq(0,1,0.001), y = predict(fit_temp, data.frame(x=seq(0,1,0.001)))), color = "purple", linewidth = 1) +
    geom_segment(x = 0.9, y = 0, xend = 0.9,
                 yend = predict(fit_temp, data.frame(x = 0.9)),
                 color = "red", linewidth = 1) +
  labs(title = paste("Dataset ", i, sep = ""))
}

#Display results
gridExtra::grid.arrange(plot_list[[1]], plot_list[[2]], plot_list[[3]], plot_list[[4]],ncol = 2)

#Illustrate Variance for Highly Inflexible (degree = 0) Model
set.seed(123)
plot_list <- list()
for(i in 1:4){
  sim_data <- gen_sim_data(f)
  
  fit_temp = lm(y ~ 1, data = sim_data)
  
  plot_list[[i]] <-
  ggplot(sim_data, mapping = aes(x=x, y = y)) +
    geom_point(alpha = 0.35) + 
    geom_line(data = data.frame(x = seq(0,1,0.001), y = predict(fit_temp, data.frame(x=seq(0,1,0.001)))), color = "orange", linewidth = 1) +
    geom_segment(x = 0.9, y = 0, xend = 0.9,
                 yend = predict(fit_temp, data.frame(x = 0.9)),
                 color = "red", linewidth = 1) +
  labs(title = paste("Dataset ", i, sep = ""))
}

#Display the results
gridExtra::grid.arrange(plot_list[[1]], plot_list[[2]], plot_list[[3]], plot_list[[4]],ncol = 2)

Simulation Study

#Initialize
set.seed(1)
n_sims <- 300
n_models <- 4
x <-  data.frame(x = 0.90) # fixed point at which we make predictions
predictions <- matrix(0, nrow = n_sims, ncol = n_models)

for (sim in 1:n_sims) {
  
  # simulate new, random, training data
  # this is the only random portion of the bias, var, and mse calculations
  # this allows us to calculate the expectation over D
  sim_data <- gen_sim_data(f)
  
  # fit models
  fit_0 <- lm(y ~ 1,                   data = sim_data)
  fit_1 <- lm(y ~ poly(x, degree = 1), data = sim_data)
  fit_2 <- lm(y ~ poly(x, degree = 2), data = sim_data)
  fit_9 <- lm(y ~ poly(x, degree = 9), data = sim_data)
  
  # get predictions
  predictions[sim, 1] <- predict(fit_0, x)
  predictions[sim, 2] <- predict(fit_1, x)
  predictions[sim, 3] <- predict(fit_2, x)
  predictions[sim, 4] <- predict(fit_9, x)
}

#Convert from wide form to long form for plotting
predictions_long <- 
  as.data.frame(predictions) %>%
  pivot_longer(cols = c(V1, V2, V3, V4), names_to = "degree", names_prefix = "V") %>%
  mutate(degree = case_when(degree == "1" ~ "0",
                            degree == "2" ~ "1",
                            degree == "3" ~ "2",
                            degree == "4" ~ "9"))

#Create plot showing predicted values for x0 = 0.9
set.seed(123) #This seed is for random jitter
ggplot(data = predictions_long, mapping = aes(x = degree, y = value)) +
  geom_boxplot(outlier.shape = NA) +
  geom_point(aes(color = degree), alpha = 0.5, shape = 1, position = "jitter") +
  geom_hline(yintercept = f(0.9), color = "black", linetype = "dashed") +
  labs(x = "Degree of Polynomial", 
       y = "Prediction") + 
  theme(legend.position="none")

#Calculate Summaries of simulation study
bias <- apply(predictions, 2, get_bias, truth = f(x = 0.90))
variance <- apply(predictions, 2, get_var)
mse <- apply(predictions, 2, get_mse, truth = f(x = 0.90))

#Display the results
sim_results <- data.frame(Degree = c(0,1,2,9),
                          MSE = mse,
                          Bias = bias,
                          BiasSquared = bias^2,
                          Variance = variance)

round(sim_results, digits = 4)
##   Degree    MSE    Bias BiasSquared Variance
## 1      0 0.2333 -0.4766      0.2271   0.0061
## 2      1 0.0222 -0.0688      0.0047   0.0175
## 3      2 0.0233  0.0130      0.0002   0.0231
## 4      9 0.0661  0.0086      0.0001   0.0660
#cbind(mse,bias^2,variance)

Example - Best Subsets Using Indirect Estimates of Test Error

Part a

#Create a copy of the dataset in the Environment
data("Credit")

#Perform Best Subsets
regfitBS <- regsubsets(Balance ~ ., data = Credit, nvmax = 11)

#Display the summary
summary(regfitBS)
## Subset selection object
## Call: regsubsets.formula(Balance ~ ., data = Credit, nvmax = 11)
## 11 Variables  (and intercept)
##             Forced in Forced out
## Income          FALSE      FALSE
## Limit           FALSE      FALSE
## Rating          FALSE      FALSE
## Cards           FALSE      FALSE
## Age             FALSE      FALSE
## Education       FALSE      FALSE
## OwnYes          FALSE      FALSE
## StudentYes      FALSE      FALSE
## MarriedYes      FALSE      FALSE
## RegionSouth     FALSE      FALSE
## RegionWest      FALSE      FALSE
## 1 subsets of each size up to 11
## Selection Algorithm: exhaustive
##           Income Limit Rating Cards Age Education OwnYes StudentYes MarriedYes
## 1  ( 1 )  " "    " "   "*"    " "   " " " "       " "    " "        " "       
## 2  ( 1 )  "*"    " "   "*"    " "   " " " "       " "    " "        " "       
## 3  ( 1 )  "*"    " "   "*"    " "   " " " "       " "    "*"        " "       
## 4  ( 1 )  "*"    "*"   " "    "*"   " " " "       " "    "*"        " "       
## 5  ( 1 )  "*"    "*"   "*"    "*"   " " " "       " "    "*"        " "       
## 6  ( 1 )  "*"    "*"   "*"    "*"   "*" " "       " "    "*"        " "       
## 7  ( 1 )  "*"    "*"   "*"    "*"   "*" " "       "*"    "*"        " "       
## 8  ( 1 )  "*"    "*"   "*"    "*"   "*" " "       "*"    "*"        " "       
## 9  ( 1 )  "*"    "*"   "*"    "*"   "*" " "       "*"    "*"        "*"       
## 10  ( 1 ) "*"    "*"   "*"    "*"   "*" " "       "*"    "*"        "*"       
## 11  ( 1 ) "*"    "*"   "*"    "*"   "*" "*"       "*"    "*"        "*"       
##           RegionSouth RegionWest
## 1  ( 1 )  " "         " "       
## 2  ( 1 )  " "         " "       
## 3  ( 1 )  " "         " "       
## 4  ( 1 )  " "         " "       
## 5  ( 1 )  " "         " "       
## 6  ( 1 )  " "         " "       
## 7  ( 1 )  " "         " "       
## 8  ( 1 )  " "         "*"       
## 9  ( 1 )  " "         "*"       
## 10  ( 1 ) "*"         "*"       
## 11  ( 1 ) "*"         "*"

Part b - Which variables are included in the best 3 variable model?

The variables included in the best 3 variable model are:

Income, Rating, StudentYes

Part c - Store the summary of the best subset selection process

#Store the summary
regfitBSsummary <- summary(regfitBS)

Part d - View, pick the best model based on BIC, plot BIC, and find variables in the model

#Part i - View the BIC values
 #similar options for rsq, rss, adjr2, and cp
regfitBSsummary$bic
##  [1]  -535.9468  -814.1798 -1173.3585 -1198.0527 -1197.0957 -1195.7321
##  [7] -1190.8790 -1185.5192 -1180.1989 -1174.9476 -1169.4433
#Part ii - Determine the size of the model associated with the best (smallest) BIC value
optSizeBIC <- which.min(regfitBSsummary$bic)
optSizeBIC
## [1] 4
#Part iii - Plot the BIC values
#First create a data frame
BICdf <- data.frame(Model = 1:nrow(regfitBSsummary$outmat), BIC = regfitBSsummary$bic)
  
#Create a plot
ggplot(data = BICdf, mapping = aes(x = Model,y = BIC)) + geom_line() + geom_point() +
  scale_x_continuous(breaks = c(1:nrow(regfitBSsummary$outmat))) + 
  geom_point(data = BICdf[optSizeBIC, ], color = "red", size = 3) +
  labs(x = "Num Features", y = "BIC")

#Part iv - Which variables remain?
coef(regfitBS, id = optSizeBIC)
##  (Intercept)       Income        Limit        Cards   StudentYes 
## -499.7272117   -7.8392288    0.2666445   23.1753794  429.6064203

Part e - View, pick the best model based on Adjusted \(R^2\), plot Adjusted \(R^2\), and find variables in the model

#Part i - View the Adjusted R^2 values
regfitBSsummary$adjr2 #similar options for rsq, rss, adjr2, and cp
##  [1] 0.7452098 0.8744888 0.9494991 0.9531099 0.9535789 0.9539961 0.9540098
##  [8] 0.9539649 0.9539243 0.9538912 0.9538287
#Part ii - Determine the size of the model associated with the best (largest) Adjusted R^2 value
optSizeAdjR2 <- which.max(regfitBSsummary$adjr2)
optSizeAdjR2
## [1] 7
#Part iii - Plot the Adjusted R^2 values
#First create a data frame
AdjR2df <- data.frame(Model = 1:nrow(regfitBSsummary$outmat),
                    AdjR2 = regfitBSsummary$adjr2)


#Create a plot
ggplot(data = AdjR2df, mapping = aes(x = Model, y = AdjR2)) +
  geom_line() +
  geom_point() +
  scale_x_continuous(breaks = c(1:11)) + 
  labs(x = "Number of Features in the Model",
       y = expression("Adjusted " * R^2 * " Value")) + #expression() is for getting exponent of 2 into title
  geom_point(data = AdjR2df[optSizeAdjR2,] , color = "red", size = 3)

#Part iv - Which variables remain?
coef(regfitBS, id = optSizeAdjR2)
##  (Intercept)       Income        Limit       Rating        Cards          Age 
## -488.6158695   -7.8036338    0.1936237    1.0940490   18.1091708   -0.6206538 
##       OwnYes   StudentYes 
##  -10.4531521  426.5812620

Example - Best Subsets Using Direct Estimates of Test Error (Validation Set Approach)

Part a - 80/20 training/validation split using a seed of 123

#Random select 80% of the row numbers to identify the training set
set.seed(123)
trainInd <- sample(1:nrow(Credit), floor(0.8*nrow(Credit)), replace = FALSE)

#Create training and validation sets
train <- Credit[trainInd, ]
validation <- Credit[ -trainInd,]

Part b - Use the best subsets approach to identify the best model of each size

#Apply the best subsets appraoch the training data
regfitBSval <- regsubsets(Balance ~ ., nvmax = 11, data = train)

#Display the summary
summary(regfitBSval)
## Subset selection object
## Call: regsubsets.formula(Balance ~ ., nvmax = 11, data = train)
## 11 Variables  (and intercept)
##             Forced in Forced out
## Income          FALSE      FALSE
## Limit           FALSE      FALSE
## Rating          FALSE      FALSE
## Cards           FALSE      FALSE
## Age             FALSE      FALSE
## Education       FALSE      FALSE
## OwnYes          FALSE      FALSE
## StudentYes      FALSE      FALSE
## MarriedYes      FALSE      FALSE
## RegionSouth     FALSE      FALSE
## RegionWest      FALSE      FALSE
## 1 subsets of each size up to 11
## Selection Algorithm: exhaustive
##           Income Limit Rating Cards Age Education OwnYes StudentYes MarriedYes
## 1  ( 1 )  " "    " "   "*"    " "   " " " "       " "    " "        " "       
## 2  ( 1 )  "*"    " "   "*"    " "   " " " "       " "    " "        " "       
## 3  ( 1 )  "*"    " "   "*"    " "   " " " "       " "    "*"        " "       
## 4  ( 1 )  "*"    "*"   " "    "*"   " " " "       " "    "*"        " "       
## 5  ( 1 )  "*"    "*"   "*"    "*"   " " " "       " "    "*"        " "       
## 6  ( 1 )  "*"    "*"   "*"    "*"   "*" " "       " "    "*"        " "       
## 7  ( 1 )  "*"    "*"   "*"    "*"   "*" "*"       " "    "*"        " "       
## 8  ( 1 )  "*"    "*"   "*"    "*"   "*" " "       " "    "*"        " "       
## 9  ( 1 )  "*"    "*"   "*"    "*"   "*" "*"       " "    "*"        " "       
## 10  ( 1 ) "*"    "*"   "*"    "*"   "*" "*"       "*"    "*"        " "       
## 11  ( 1 ) "*"    "*"   "*"    "*"   "*" "*"       "*"    "*"        "*"       
##           RegionSouth RegionWest
## 1  ( 1 )  " "         " "       
## 2  ( 1 )  " "         " "       
## 3  ( 1 )  " "         " "       
## 4  ( 1 )  " "         " "       
## 5  ( 1 )  " "         " "       
## 6  ( 1 )  " "         " "       
## 7  ( 1 )  " "         " "       
## 8  ( 1 )  "*"         "*"       
## 9  ( 1 )  "*"         "*"       
## 10  ( 1 ) "*"         "*"       
## 11  ( 1 ) "*"         "*"

Part c - Create and display the data matrix for validation set

#Create data matrix
validationMat <- model.matrix(Balance ~ ., data = validation)

#Display first 6 rows
head(validationMat, 6)
##    (Intercept)  Income Limit Rating Cards Age Education OwnYes StudentYes
## 1            1  14.891  3606    283     2  34        11      0          0
## 3            1 104.593  7075    514     4  71        11      0          0
## 6            1  80.180  8047    569     4  77        10      0          0
## 15           1  19.144  3291    269     2  75        13      1          0
## 17           1  53.598  3714    286     3  73        17      1          0
## 18           1  36.496  4378    339     3  69        15      1          0
##    MarriedYes RegionSouth RegionWest
## 1           1           1          0
## 3           0           0          1
## 6           0           1          0
## 15          0           0          0
## 17          1           0          0
## 18          1           0          1

Part d - Use a loop to obtain predictions and MSE for validation set for optimal model of each size

#Initialize 
numModels <- ncol(validationMat) - 1 
valMSE <- rep(NA, numModels) #Repeats NA numModels times (11 in this problem)

#Loop to cycle through the models of different sizes
for (i in 1:numModels){
  coefi <- coef(regfitBSval, id = i) #Extract coefficients for model with i variables
  yhat <- validationMat[, names(coefi)] %*% coefi #yhat = X %*% betahat
  valMSE[i] <- mean((validation$Balance - yhat)^2) #Calculate and store val. set mse
}

#Display validation set MSE values
valMSE
##  [1] 42670.587 17025.339  9766.416  9116.494  8931.049  8682.380  8760.901
##  [8]  8760.359  8849.534  8766.308  8716.232
#Determine which size produced the optimal
optSizeVal <- which.min(valMSE)
optSizeVal
## [1] 6
#Create a plot of Validation Set MSE's
#First create a data frame
valMSEdf <- data.frame(Model = 1:numModels,
                       MSE = valMSE)

#Create a plot of Validation Set MSE's
ggplot(data = valMSEdf, mapping = aes(x = Model, y = MSE)) +
  geom_line() +
  geom_point() +
  scale_x_continuous(breaks = c(1:11)) + 
  labs(x = "Number of Features in the Model",
       y = "Validation Set MSE") +
  geom_point(data = valMSEdf[optSizeVal,] , color = "red", size = 3)

#Display the coefficients of the optimal model (coefficients were estimated using train)
coef(regfitBS, id = optSizeVal)
##  (Intercept)       Income        Limit       Rating        Cards          Age 
## -493.7341870   -7.7950824    0.1936914    1.0911874   18.2118976   -0.6240560 
##   StudentYes 
##  425.6099369

Example - Applying Shrinkage Methods in R

Part a - Preparing the data for glmnet

#Create a copy of Credit in the Environment
data("Credit")

#Create input matrix and response vector
Xmat <- model.matrix(Balance ~ ., data = Credit)[, -1]
yvec <- Credit$Balance


#Perform training/validation split
set.seed(1)
trainInd <- sample(1:nrow(Credit), floor(0.8*nrow(Credit)), replace = FALSE)

set.seed(NULL)
#Split Xmat and yvec separately
XmatTrain <- Xmat[trainInd,]
XmatVal <- Xmat[-trainInd,]
yvecTrain <- yvec[trainInd]
yvecVal <- yvec[-trainInd]

Part b - Build the LASSO model and plot the coefficient paths

#Fit the LASSO model (let R choice lambda sequence)
lassoModel <- glmnet(x = XmatTrain, y = yvecTrain,
                    family = "gaussian",
                    alpha = 1,
                    lambda = NULL,
                    standardize = TRUE)

#Create a plot of the coefficient paths
plot(lassoModel, xvar = "lambda", label = TRUE)

Part c - Build the Ridge model and plot the coefficient paths

#Fit the Ridge model (let R choice lambda sequence)
ridgeModel <- glmnet(x = XmatTrain, y = yvecTrain,
                     family = "gaussian",
                     alpha = 0, 
                     lambda = NULL,
                     standardize = TRUE)

#Create a plot of the coefficient paths
plot(ridgeModel, xvar = "lambda", label = TRUE)

Part d - For the LASSO models Extract 44th value of lambda and associated coefficients

#Extract the 44th value of lambda
lassoModel$lambda[44]
## [1] 7.334662
#Calculate the log of the 44th value of lambda (doing this since the plot of coefficient paths used log(lambda))
log(lassoModel$lambda[44])
## [1] 1.992611
#Obtain coefficients associated with 44th value of lambda
predict(lassoModel, s = lassoModel$lambda[44], type = "coefficients")
## 12 x 1 sparse Matrix of class "dgCMatrix"
##               s=7.334662
## (Intercept) -474.4506930
## Income        -6.7473718
## Limit          0.1644503
## Rating         1.3219277
## Cards         12.6194866
## Age           -0.4582826
## Education      .        
## OwnYes         .        
## StudentYes   384.8676498
## MarriedYes     .        
## RegionSouth    .        
## RegionWest     .
#Add vertical line at 44th lambda on coefficients plot
plot(lassoModel, xvar = "lambda", label = TRUE)
abline(v = log(lassoModel$lambda[44]), col = "red")

Part e - Pick Optimal Value of Lambda

#Use 10-fold CV to pick lambda for LASSO
set.seed(123)
lassoCV <- cv.glmnet(x = XmatTrain, y = yvecTrain,
                    family = "gaussian",
                    alpha = 1, 
                    lambda = NULL,
                    standardize = TRUE,
                    nfolds = 10)
set.seed(NULL)

#Show the LASSO CV results
plot(lassoCV)

#Use 10-fold CV to pick lambda for Ridge
set.seed(123)
ridgeCV <- cv.glmnet(x = XmatTrain, y = yvecTrain,
                    family = "gaussian",
                    alpha = 0, 
                    lambda = NULL,
                    standardize = TRUE,
                    nfolds = 10)
set.seed(NULL)

#Show the Ridge CV results
plot(ridgeCV)

Part f - Display the optimal values of lambda and the associated coefficients

#Display the optimal values of lambda
lassoCV$lambda.min
## [1] 1.039669
lassoCV$lambda.1se
## [1] 7.334662
#Store the coefficients associated with the optimal values
coefLamMin <- predict(lassoCV, s = lassoCV$lambda.min, type = "coefficients")
coefLam1se <- predict(lassoCV, s = lassoCV$lambda.1se, type = "coefficients")

#Create a data frame for comparing the coefficients
tempdf <- 
  data.frame(Variable = row.names(coefLamMin), 
             lamMin = as.numeric(coefLamMin), 
             lam1se = as.numeric(coefLam1se))

tempdf
##       Variable       lamMin       lam1se
## 1  (Intercept) -493.8077797 -474.4506930
## 2       Income   -7.6482818   -6.7473718
## 3        Limit    0.1981479    0.1644503
## 4       Rating    1.0277416    1.3219277
## 5        Cards   18.9749073   12.6194866
## 6          Age   -0.6285526   -0.4582826
## 7    Education    0.0000000    0.0000000
## 8       OwnYes  -13.8549370    0.0000000
## 9   StudentYes  411.5126361  384.8676498
## 10  MarriedYes   -4.4501043    0.0000000
## 11 RegionSouth    0.0000000    0.0000000
## 12  RegionWest    8.2124313    0.0000000

Part g - Obtain Preditions for Validation Set and calculate RMSE

#LASSO
lassoYhat <- predict(lassoCV, s = lassoCV$lambda.min, newx = XmatVal) 
lassoMSE <-  mean((yvecVal - lassoYhat)^2)
lassoRMSE <- sqrt(lassoMSE)
lassoRMSE

#Ridge
ridgeYhat <- predict(ridgeCV, s = ridgeCV$lambda.min, newx = XmatVal) 
ridgeMSE <-  mean((yvecVal - ridgeYhat)^2)
ridgeRMSE <- sqrt(ridgeMSE)
ridgeRMSE


#Least Squares (train and validation are not the same as we used in cv.glmnet)
train <- Credit[trainInd, ]
validation <- Credit[-trainInd,]
lsModel <- lm(Balance ~ ., data = train)
lsYhat <- predict(lsModel, newdata = validation)

lsMSE <- mean((validation$Balance - lsYhat)^2)
lsRMSE <- sqrt(lsMSE)
lsRMSE
library(ISLR2)
library(leaps)
data("Auto")
Auto2 <- Auto[, !(names(Auto) %in% c("origin", "name"))]
Auto2 <- subset(Auto, select = -c(origin, name))
regfit.full <- regsubsets(acceleration ~ ., data = Auto2, nvmax = 7)
reg.summary <- summary(regfit.full)
reg.summary
## Subset selection object
## Call: regsubsets.formula(acceleration ~ ., data = Auto2, nvmax = 7)
## 6 Variables  (and intercept)
##              Forced in Forced out
## mpg              FALSE      FALSE
## cylinders        FALSE      FALSE
## displacement     FALSE      FALSE
## horsepower       FALSE      FALSE
## weight           FALSE      FALSE
## year             FALSE      FALSE
## 1 subsets of each size up to 6
## Selection Algorithm: exhaustive
##          mpg cylinders displacement horsepower weight year
## 1  ( 1 ) " " " "       " "          "*"        " "    " " 
## 2  ( 1 ) " " " "       " "          "*"        "*"    " " 
## 3  ( 1 ) " " " "       "*"          "*"        "*"    " " 
## 4  ( 1 ) " " " "       "*"          "*"        "*"    "*" 
## 5  ( 1 ) " " "*"       "*"          "*"        "*"    "*" 
## 6  ( 1 ) "*" "*"       "*"          "*"        "*"    "*"
library(ISLR2)
library(leaps)

data("Auto")
Auto2 <- subset(Auto, select = -c(origin, name))
regfit.full <- regsubsets(acceleration ~ ., data = Auto2, nvmax = 7)
reg.summary <- summary(regfit.full)
which.min(reg.summary$bic)
## [1] 3
reg.summary$which[which.min(reg.summary$bic), ]
##  (Intercept)          mpg    cylinders displacement   horsepower       weight 
##         TRUE        FALSE        FALSE         TRUE         TRUE         TRUE 
##         year 
##        FALSE

```
